era_context: The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Ma'mun continues its intellectual and military consolidation. The Tang Dynasty is in the final decades of its effective central authority, with provincial military governors (jiedushi) increasingly autonomous. The Byzantine Empire under Theophilus I maintains iconoclastic policies. In Western Europe, the Carolingian Empire under Louis the Pious is experiencing internal tensions that will lead to civil war after his death in 840. This is a period of intellectual ferment in the Islamic world, military fragmentation in China, and political instability across Eurasia.

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun's military campaign against Byzantine Empire
events.description: Al-Ma'mun launches a major military campaign against the Byzantine Empire, continuing the long-standing Arab–Byzantine frontier conflicts. The campaign targets Byzantine territories in Anatolia and aims to reassert Abbasid military dominance. These raids and campaigns are part of the broader pattern of Abbasid–Byzantine competition for control of the eastern Mediterranean and Anatolia. The campaign reflects al-Ma'mun's military ambitions and the ongoing religious and political rivalry between the Caliphate and the Empire.
key_figures: Abbasid military commanders (names vary by source)
events.certainty_note: Abbasid–Byzantine military campaigns during al-Ma'mun's reign are well-attested, but specific dating to 829 is uncertain. The campaign may have occurred in 829, 830, or nearby years. Dating is approximate within the broader period of al-Ma'mun's reign (813–833).
cross_references: Part of broader Abbasid–Byzantine frontier conflicts

events.title: Tang Dynasty military fragmentation and provincial autonomy
events.description: The Tang Dynasty continues to experience erosion of central authority as provincial military governors (jiedushi) consolidate power and resist imperial control. By 829, the central government's ability to command provincial armies is severely compromised. This fragmentation is a key factor in the Tang Dynasty's decline and will eventually contribute to its collapse in 907. The period is marked by internal military tensions and the rise of powerful regional strongmen.
key_figures: Emperor Jingzong of Tang (r. 824–826) or Emperor Wenzong of Tang (r. 827–840) — depending on exact dating
events.certainty_note: Tang military fragmentation in the 820s–830s is well-documented in later sources. The process was gradual and continuous, so dating to a specific year is somewhat arbitrary, but 829 falls within the well-attested period of provincial autonomy.
cross_references: Part of broader Tang decline (8th–9th centuries)
Precedes An Lushan Rebellion aftermath and final Tang collapse (907)

events.title: Carolingian Empire under Louis the Pious experiences succession tensions
events.description: Emperor Louis the Pious (r. 814–840) faces increasing pressure from his sons regarding the division of the Carolingian Empire. Tensions over succession and the distribution of territories among his heirs (Lothair, Louis the German, and Charles the Bald) are mounting. These disputes will lead to civil war after Louis's death in 840 and the eventual fragmentation of the empire. The year 829 marks a period of growing instability within the Frankish realm.

sources.name: Astronomer, Life of Louis the Pious (9th century)

events.certainty_note: The succession tensions under Louis the Pious are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary Frankish sources. The year 829 falls within the documented period of growing discord.
cross_references: Precedes civil war after Louis's death (840)
Leads to Treaty of Verdun (843) and fragmentation of Carolingian Empire

events.title: Byzantine Emperor Theophilus I continues iconoclastic policies
events.description: Theophilus I continues the second iconoclastic period, persecuting icon veneration and monks who resist the ban on religious images. This represents the continuation of theological conflict that has divided the Byzantine Church for over a century. Theophilus is known for harsh measures against iconodule monks, including imprisonment and torture. The policy will be reversed after his death in 842.

sources.name: Life of Saint Theodore of Studion (hagiographic source)

events.certainty_note: Theophilus's reign and iconoclastic policies are well-documented. The year 829 falls within his reign (829–842), and persecution of iconodules is attested throughout this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The specific dating of Abbasid–Byzantine military campaigns to 829 is uncertain; sources often conflate campaigns from the 820s–830s. The significance of Tang provincial fragmentation in 829 specifically is debatable; the process was gradual and continuous, and no single year marks a decisive shift. The Carolingian succession tensions are well-documented, but the year 829 is somewhat arbitrary as a marker; tensions had been building since at least the 820s.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 829 CE is moderate, with similar biases as 830. Abbasid and Byzantine sources are abundant but often compiled centuries later. Frankish sources (Royal Frankish Annals, Astronomer's Life of Louis) are more contemporary but reflect court ideology. Tang sources are later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are absent from contemporary written records. The surviving sources reflect elite, urban, literate cultures.

graph_edges.note: Abbasid military campaigns and intellectual patronage occurred simultaneously under al-Ma'mun.
Succession tensions under Louis the Pious led to civil war after his death in 840.
Tang provincial fragmentation in 829 was part of the broader process leading to the dynasty's collapse in 907.
